title: Ask and Embla
summary: In the time before time, when the world was young and the great void of Ginnungagap had only recently given birth to the first living things, Odin and his brothers Vili and Vé walked along the shore of the newly made world.
full_text:
In the time before time, when the world was young and the great void of Ginnungagap had only recently given birth to the first living things, Odin and his brothers Vili and Vé walked along the shore of the newly made world. The sea lapped against a land still raw from creation, still bearing the scars of Ymir's death, from whose body the earth itself had been fashioned.

As they walked, the three gods came upon two trees lying on the sand—driftwood carried by the primordial tides. One was an ash, strong and straight, its grain tight and its heartwood dense. The other was an elm, graceful and supple, its branches shaped by the winds of nine worlds. They were beautiful in their way, these trees, but they were empty of spirit, empty of consciousness, empty of the fire that separates the living from the merely existing.

The three brothers looked upon the trees and saw that they were shaped like themselves—like gods. And a thought passed between them, wordless and divine: Let us make something new.

Odin knelt first. He breathed into the ash tree his own breath—önd, the spirit of life. The wood trembled, warmed, and began to stir. Vili reached out next and bestowed óðr, the gift of intellect and passion, the wild energy that drives the mind to seek and to feel. The form on the sand shuddered with the first spark of awareness. Then Vé, the third brother, gave the gifts of form and senses—hearing and sight, speech and touch, the warmth of blood and the flush of healthy skin.

The ash tree rose as a man, and they named him Askr. The elm tree rose as a woman, and they named her Embla. They stood together on the shore of the new world, naked and innocent, filled with the breath of Odin, the mind of Vili, and the senses of Vé. The gods clothed them and gave them a home—Midgard, the Middle Realm, the world of humans, walled safely against the chaos of giants.

From Ask and Embla came all the races of humanity. Their children spread across Midgard, building homes and raising families, telling stories by firelight, looking up at the stars and wondering about the gods who had shaped them from wood and breath.

But the gifts of the gods carried weight. Breath required food—and so humans must labor. Spirit brought worry—and so humans must endure. Consciousness brought knowledge—and so humans must choose between wisdom and folly, good and evil. And that first breath, that gift of önd, was finite. Every human who draws it must one day release it back to the gods.

This is why, the old skalds say, we work and weep and wonder. Because the gods gave us everything—including the burden of being mortal, the weight of being free. Every child born carries within them the memory of the ash and the elm, the salt of the primordial sea, the breath of Odin himself. We are made from the world tree, rooted in the earth, reaching for the sky, and one day, returning to the soil from which we came. But in between—in the brief, blazing moment of being alive—we are the gods' greatest creation: beings who know they will die, and love life all the more because of it.
moral: With gifts come responsibilities.

source_references.title: The Poetic Edda
source_references.author: Carolyne Larrington (trans.)
source_references.notes: Völuspá st. 17–18: creation of Ask and Embla by Odin, Vili, and Vé

title: Anansi and the Pot of Wisdom
summary: Anansi tricks humans into giving him all the world's wisdom—but discovers it cannot be kept.
full_text:
In the beginning, before the first story was ever told, all the wisdom in the world belonged to Nyame, the Sky God. He kept it sealed in a clay pot, far above the clouds, where no human or creature could reach it. But Nyame looked down one day and saw that the people of the earth stumbled through their lives in ignorance, and his heart softened. He decided to send his wisdom into the world, and he chose Anansi the spider to be its keeper.

Anansi was small and clever, with eight nimble legs and a mind that spun schemes faster than silk. He was known throughout the villages as the one who could talk his way out of any trap and trick his way into any feast. When Nyame lowered the great clay pot on a golden thread and placed it before Anansi, the spider's many eyes glittered with delight.

"Take this pot," Nyame said, his voice like distant thunder. "It contains everything there is to know: the secrets of the stars, the language of the animals, the art of growing yams, the songs of the wind. Share it with every creature, so that all may live in wisdom."

Anansi bowed and promised to obey. But as he carried the pot home, he felt its weight and heard the whispers of knowledge shifting inside. He thought of how everyone would praise him if he alone possessed all this wisdom. "Why should I give it away?" he muttered. "If I am the only wise one, they will all bow to me."

So Anansi decided to hide the pot at the top of the tallest tree in the forest, where no one could ever find it. He wound a strong vine around the pot, tied it to his belly, and began to climb. But the pot was heavy and round, and it swung against his knees and chest with every movement. He scrambled upward a few inches, then slipped. He grasped at the bark and scraped his legs, but the pot kept banging into the trunk, throwing him off balance. Again and again he tried, his eight legs flailing, but the pot always got in the way.

Down below, hidden behind a fern, stood Anansi's youngest son, Ntikuma. He had followed his father out of curiosity and had watched the whole struggle. He saw the pot swinging against Anansi's belly and saw how it knocked him off balance each time. At last, he could not keep silent.

"Father!" Ntikuma called out. "Why do you not tie the pot to your back instead? Then your arms and legs would be free to climb."

Anansi froze halfway up the tree. The boy's words struck him like a bolt of lightning. He had a pot full of all the world's wisdom, yet a child had just shown him something he had not thought of himself. What use was all this wisdom if a mere boy could outsmart him?

Rage and shame flooded through Anansi's eight legs. He tore the pot from his belly and hurled it down with all his strength. The clay pot hit a rock and shattered into a thousand pieces. The wisdom inside burst forth like a rainbow of light, scattering in every direction. Some of it rode the wind to distant villages, where it settled in the hearts of farmers and fishermen. Some of it sank into the rivers and trees. Some of it fell into the mouths of children and elders alike. From that day forward, no single creature possessed all the wisdom of the world. Instead, every person carried a small piece of it. And that is why we must listen to each other, even the youngest among us, because wisdom can come from anywhere and anyone. Anansi learned that day that knowledge hoarded is knowledge lost, but wisdom shared grows beyond measure.
moral: Wisdom cannot be hoarded—it grows when shared.

title: Aladdin and the Magic Lamp
summary: In a far city of China, though the tale itself comes from the sands of Arabia, there lived a poor tailor named Mustapha and his wife, who had one son.
full_text:
In a far city of China, though the tale itself comes from the sands of Arabia, there lived a poor tailor named Mustapha and his wife, who had one son. That son was Aladdin, and he was, by all accounts, a hopeless scoundrel. From morning till night he ran through the streets with other idle boys, playing knuckle-bones and dice, and refusing every attempt his father made to teach him the tailor's trade. His father died of grief, and still Aladdin did not mend his ways.

One day, when Aladdin was fifteen, a stranger approached him in the street. He was a tall man with a dark face and piercing eyes, and he wore the robes of a magician from the distant land of Morocco. He claimed to be Aladdin's long-lost uncle, returned after forty years abroad. Aladdin's mother, poor and desperate, believed the lie. The stranger showered them with gifts and promises.

The magician led Aladdin deep into the mountains beyond the city gates, through gardens and rocky valleys, until they reached a narrow gorge between two peaks. There the magician lit a fire, threw strange powders into the flames, and muttered incantations that made the earth tremble. The ground split open, revealing a stone slab with a brass ring. Terror seized Aladdin, but the magician struck him and said: "Obey me, and you will find a treasure greater than any king possesses."

Aladdin lifted the stone and saw steps descending into darkness. The magician gave him a ring and told him to fetch an old oil lamp from a niche at the bottom of the cave. Aladdin descended through three vast halls filled with gold, crossed a garden of jewel-laden trees, and found the lamp. But as he climbed back, the magician demanded the lamp be handed up first. Suspicious, Aladdin refused. The magician flew into a fury, sealed the cave entrance with magic, and left Aladdin trapped in the darkness.

For three days Aladdin sat in despair. At last, in desperation, he rubbed the ring the magician had given him. A monstrous genie appeared, filling the cavern with his presence. "I am the Slave of the Ring," the genie boomed. "Command me." Aladdin asked to be taken home. In an instant, he stood in his mother's house.

When his mother tried to clean the old lamp to sell it, she rubbed it, and a far greater genie emerged, towering to the ceiling. "I am the Slave of the Lamp," said the genie. "Your wish is my command." Aladdin asked for food, and a feast fit for a sultan appeared on silver platters.

Over time, Aladdin used the lamp to lift his family from poverty. One day he saw the Sultan's daughter, Princess Badroulboudour, and fell hopelessly in love. He sent his mother to the palace with jewels from the garden of the cave, and the Sultan, dazzled by such wealth, agreed to the marriage. The lamp's genie built Aladdin a magnificent palace of marble and jade, with twenty-four windows studded with rubies and emeralds.

But the Moroccan magician learned of Aladdin's fortune. Disguised as a merchant trading old lamps for new, he tricked the princess into exchanging the magic lamp. The genie transported the entire palace to Africa. Aladdin, heartbroken, used the ring genie to travel to his wife. Together they plotted. The princess feigned welcome to the magician, drugged his wine, and Aladdin killed him. Recovering the lamp, Aladdin brought his palace and princess back to China. They lived in peace and prosperity, and Aladdin proved that even a worthless boy could become a great man, not through magic alone, but through courage and cleverness.
moral: Goodness wins prosperity.

source_references.title: Aladdin and the Magic Lamp: A Study of the Arabian Nights
source_references.author: Paulo Lemos Horta

source_references.title: The Arabian Nights Encyclopedia
source_references.author: Ulrich Marzolph and Richard van Leeuwen

title: Ali Baba and the Forty Thieves
summary: In a town in Persia, there once lived two brothers.. The elder, Cassim, married a wealthy woman and became the richest merchant in the marketplace.
full_text:
In a town in Persia, there once lived two brothers. The elder, Cassim, married a wealthy woman and became the richest merchant in the marketplace. The younger, Ali Baba, married a woman as poor as himself and earned his living by cutting wood in the forest and selling it from the backs of three donkeys.

One afternoon, as Ali Baba was loading his donkeys with firewood, he saw a cloud of dust rising on the horizon. A band of horsemen approached at full gallop, their saddlebags bulging. Fearing they were robbers, Ali Baba climbed a large tree and concealed himself among the branches. From his hiding place, he counted forty men. They dismounted before a massive rock face, and their captain strode forward and called out: "Open, Sesame!"

To Ali Baba's astonishment, a door swung open in the solid rock. The forty thieves entered, and the door closed behind them. After a time, they emerged, remounted their horses, and rode away. When the coast was clear, Ali Baba climbed down, approached the rock, and spoke the magic words. The door opened into a cavern filled with gold coins, silken fabrics, gem-encrusted weapons, and sacks of silver. He loaded his donkeys with as much gold as they could carry and hurried home.

Ali Baba's wife needed to measure the gold, so she borrowed a measuring cup from Cassim's household. Cassim's wife, curious about her poor sister-in-law's sudden need for a measure, secretly smeared the bottom of the cup with lard. When the cup was returned, a gold coin was stuck to it. Cassim confronted Ali Baba and forced him to reveal the secret of the cave.

The greedy Cassim rode to the cave with ten mules. Inside, he filled sack after sack with treasure. But when he tried to leave, the magic words would not come to his lips. In his excitement, he had forgotten them. "Open, Barley!" he cried. "Open, Wheat!" The rock stayed shut. The forty thieves returned, found him inside, and cut his body into quarters as a warning.

When Cassim did not return, Ali Baba went to the cave and found his brother's remains. He brought them home and entrusted them to Morgiana, his brother's clever slave-girl. Morgiana was as quick-witted as she was loyal. She found an old cobbler named Baba Mustapha, blindfolded him, and led him to the house to sew the quarters of Cassim's body back together, so that a proper burial could be held without arousing suspicion.

The thieves, finding the body gone, knew someone else shared their secret. One by one, they tracked down the cobbler and, by offering gold, convinced him to retrace his steps. The first thief marked Ali Baba's door with chalk, but Morgiana spotted the mark and chalked every door in the street. The second thief used red chalk, and Morgiana did the same. Both thieves were killed by their captain for failure.

Finally, the captain himself memorized the location and devised a plan. He disguised himself as an oil merchant and arrived at Ali Baba's house with forty mules, each carrying a large clay jar. Thirty-nine of the jars concealed armed thieves; only one held oil. The captain begged shelter for the night.

Morgiana, always watchful, noticed something strange. When her oil lamp ran low, she went to the jars. The first thief inside whispered, "Is it time?" Morgiana, realizing the plot, answered in a low voice: "Not yet." She saw the danger. She filled a great cauldron with oil, boiled it, and poured enough into each jar to kill every hidden thief. The captain, discovering his men dead, fled into the night.

Later, the captain returned disguised as a merchant and befriended Ali Baba's son. He was invited to dinner. Morgiana recognized him despite the disguise and, during the feast, performed a dagger dance that ended with the captain's heart stilled. For her courage and loyalty, Ali Baba freed Morgiana and married her to his son. Together they lived in prosperity, and Ali Baba was the only man alive who knew the words that opened the cave.
moral: Greed brings danger.

source_references.title: The Thousand and One Nights: Space, Travel and Transformation
source_references.author: Marina Warner

title: Adze: The Vampiric Firefly
summary: A witch-spirit takes firefly form, entering homes to drain the blood of sleepers.
full_text:
In the lush, shadowed forests of Ghana, among the Ewe people, there is a terror that does not hunt with claws or teeth, but with a deceptive, flickering light. They call it the Adze—the vampiric firefly.

The Adze is not a creature of nature, but a product of malice. It is a witch-spirit, a parasite of the soul that emerges from its human host at night. When the sun dips below the horizon and the forest becomes a wall of ink, the Adze leaves the body of the witch who controls it, transforming into a small, glowing firefly. In this form, it is almost beautiful, a dancing spark of light that seems to promise wonder or guidance.

But the light of the Adze is a lure. It seeks out the vulnerable—the sleeping child, the exhausted traveler, the isolated elder. It enters homes through the smallest of cracks, gliding through keyholes and gaps in the thatch with a ghostly silence. Once inside, the Adze reveals its true, parasitic nature. It does not bite like a bat or a wolf; instead, it uses a magical, invisible straw to drain the blood of its victim while they sleep.

The attack is subtle. The victim does not wake; they only feel a slight chill, a vague sense of dread. But as the nights pass, the Adze returns again and again. The victim grows pale, their eyes sunken, their strength vanishing as if leaked into the air. The village elders recognize the signs—this is not a sickness of the body, but a theft of the life-force. The Adze is often hired by the envious or the jealous, those who cannot bear the success of their neighbors and pay a witch to ruin them from the shadows.

The terror of the Adze is that it is an invisible enemy. You cannot fight a firefly with a sword, and you cannot argue with a spirit. The only defense is spiritual warfare. To detect the Adze, the Ewe people use the bones of a python, burning them in a ritual fire; the smoke is said to force the Adze to reveal its human form or flee in agony. To protect a home, they burn the bones of a monkey at the threshold, creating a barrier of scent and energy that the firefly cannot cross.

The legend of the Adze is a reflection of the social anxieties of the community. It is a story about the hidden dangers of envy and the way hatred can travel in a form that looks harmless. It teaches that the most dangerous predators are not those who roar in the jungle, but those who smile in the daylight and hunt in the form of a flickering light at midnight. In the forests of Ghana, when a single firefly dances too close to the window, the people close their shutters tight and pray that the light is merely a light.
moral: Envy directed becomes spiritual attack.

title: Anansi Boys
summary: Anansi's sons—Fat Face and Small Head—continue their fathers trickery in the Caribbean.
full_text:
In the vibrant, sun-soaked markets and colorful streets of Jamaica, the air is always filled with music, laughter, and the art of the "trick." This spirit of cleverness is the legacy of Anansi, the great spider-god of West Africa who traveled across the ocean in the hearts of the enslaved, becoming a symbol of survival and resistance in the Caribbean.

But Anansi did not leave behind just stories; he left behind his sons—Fat Face and Small Head. Though they lacked their father's eight legs, they inherited his eight-fold cunning. Fat Face and Small Head were not interested in the grand designs of gods; they were interested in the immediate pleasure of a full belly and a small fortune.

One afternoon, the brothers arrived at the gates of a wealthy plantation owner's estate. The man was known for his greed and his disdain for the poor, but he had a weakness for the exotic and the miraculous. "Sir!" Small Head cried, his voice a symphony of faux-excitement. "We are the world's greatest circus performers! We possess magic that would make the stars dance! Pay us a handful of gold, and we shall show you a miracle."

Intrigued, the rich man agreed. He led them to a courtyard and waited. Fat Face sat upon a simple wooden bucket, looking solemn and focused. Small Head stepped forward and, with a flourish of his hands and a loud "Hocus Pocus!", flipped the bucket over. "Behold!" he shouted. "Now there are TWO buckets!"

The rich man blinked. He looked at the two buckets—one on top of the other—and for a moment, his greed clouded his judgment. He was so eager to be fooled that he actually believed he had witnessed a duplication of matter. He cheered and tossed them a gold coin.

But the brothers were not done. Small Head stood on his head, balancing perfectly. "And now, I shall walk upon the air with my feet in the clouds!" he claimed. As he performed a clumsy flip, his trousers fell down, revealing a pair of brightly colored undergarments. The crowd burst into laughter, and the rich man, finally realizing he had been played, chased them from his land with a cane.

The brothers ran, laughing all the way, their gold coins jingling in their pockets. They eventually sought refuge with a local priest, confessing their trickery with a wink. The priest, amused by their audacity, forgave them, noting that they had simply applied the "Anansi Method" to a man who deserved to be humbled.

The stories of the Anansi Boys are more than just comedies; they are lessons in survival. In a world where the powerful often hold all the cards, wit is the only weapon available to the marginalized. Fat Face and Small Head represent the enduring human spirit—the ability to find a loophole in the system, to laugh in the face of oppression, and to turn a simple bucket into a source of freedom. They remind us that while strength can build a wall, only cleverness can find the way over it.
moral: Wit survives all—use your brains.

source_references.title: Anansi the Spider: A Tale from the Ashanti
source_references.author: Gerald McDermott

title: Aswang: The Shapeshifter
summary: The most feared creature in Philippine folklore—a shapeshifter that eats human flesh, especially unborn children.
full_text:
In the quietest corners of the Philippine archipelago, where the mangroves tangle into impenetrable walls and the jungles breathe with a heavy, humid heat, there exists a name that is spoken only in whispers: the Aswang. It is not a single creature, but a nightmare collective—an umbrella term for a host of shapeshifting monstrosities that haunt the periphery of human civilization, waiting for the sun to dip below the horizon.

By day, the Aswang is a master of camouflage. They are the unassuming neighbors, the shy vendors in the market, or the solitary travelers who arrive in town with a polite smile and a distant gaze. They blend into the social fabric of the barangay with an eerie perfection, their presence unnoticed until the same hour every night when the wind shifts and the dogs begin to howl in a panic that borders on madness.

As midnight approaches, the mask of humanity slips. Depending on the nature of the specific entity, the Aswang may transform into a massive black dog, a feral cat, or a bloated pig. Some are more grotesque, becoming winged horrors that screech across the moonlight, their cries sounding like the wind through a hollow bone. Their hunger is not for ordinary food, but for the raw, pulsing energy of human flesh—specifically the organs of the healthy and the unborn.

Their most feared trait is their predatory instinct for pregnancy. An Aswang can scent a developing fetus from miles away, drawn to the scent of new life like a shark to blood. They are the architects of the 'night-terror,' slipping through cracks in the walls or descending from the thatch roofs with a needle-like tongue to steal the life of a child before it is even born. To the victims, it is a sudden, inexplicable illness; to the Aswang, it is a feast of the highest order.

But the Aswang is a creature of patterns and weaknesses. To the observant, the signs are there: the neighbor who never eats in public, the woman whose eyes reflect the world upside down, or the dog whose tail is suspiciously short. The villagers have developed a desperate arsenal of countermeasures to survive the night. They keep bowls of salt and crushed garlic by the door, for the Aswang finds these pungent scents intolerable. They use the 'buntot pagi'—the whip made from the tail of a stingray—which can tear through the Aswang's supernatural hide and force it back into human form.

There is a darker side to the Aswang's existence: the cycle of infection. It is believed that the power of the Aswang is passed through a lapped wound or a specific type of food spat upon by an elder monster. Once a human is 'marked,' they are slowly consumed by the hunger, their morality eroding until they, too, become a predator of their own kin.

The legend of the Aswang serves as a grim reminder of the fragility of trust. It tells us that the most dangerous monsters are not those that hide in the caves, but those that sit beside us at the dinner table, hiding their fangs behind a smile, waiting for the moon to rise and the hunger to take hold.
moral: Monsters walk among us—recognize them.

source_references.title: Aswang and Other Mythical Creatures of the Philippines
source_references.author: C.J.S. Zialcita

source_references.title: Philippine Folklore: Aswang in Myth and Reality
source_references.author: Maximo Ramos

title: Amaterasu and the Cave
summary: Amaterasu, the Sun Goddess of Shinto, is the most important deity in the Japanese pantheon.
full_text:
Amaterasu, the Sun Goddess of Shinto, is the most important deity in the Japanese pantheon. Her name means "Shining in Heaven," and she is said to be the direct ancestor of the Japanese imperial family. But even a sun goddess has her moments of darkness.

Amaterasu lived in the celestial palace of Takamagahara with her siblings: Tsukuyomi, the moon god, and Susanoo, the storm god. Susanoo was wild and unpredictable, a force of chaos in the orderly heavens. He destroyed Amaterasu's rice fields, defiled her weaving hall, and threw a flayed horse through the roof of her sacred chamber.

Amaterasu was heartbroken and furious. She retreated into the Heavenly Rock Cave, called the Ama-no-Iwato. She sealed the entrance with a massive boulder. With the sun goddess hidden, the world fell into perpetual darkness. Crops withered, the gods stumbled blindly, and evil spirits roamed freely.

The eight million gods of Shinto gathered outside the cave. They tried every trick to lure Amaterasu out. They offered her jewels, food, and prayers. Nothing worked. Finally, the goddess Ame-no-Uzume, the spirit of revelry, devised a plan. She turned over a tub, climbed on top, and began a wild, lewd dance. The other gods laughed and cheered. The noise was so great that Amaterasu grew curious.

"What is happening?" she asked from inside the cave.

"We have found a goddess more beautiful than you," Ame-no-Uzume replied. "Come and see."

Jealous and intrigued, Amaterasu cracked the boulder open a sliver. In that instant, the god Ame-no-Tajikarao threw the boulder aside, and the goddess Ishikoridome held up a mirror. Amaterasu saw her own reflection for the first time. She was so dazzled by her own beauty that she stepped forward — and the world was flooded with light once more.

Amaterasu's return from the cave is celebrated every year in Shinto rituals. The mirror she saw is preserved at the Grand Shrine of Ise, the holiest site in Japan. The story is a reminder that even the sun can be obscured by sorrow, but light always returns — and sometimes, laughter is the force that brings it back.
moral: Self-reflection reveals inner light.

source_references.title: Kojiki: A New Translation of an Ancient Chronicle of Japan
source_references.author: Donald Philippi (trans.)

title: Anubis
summary: Anubis is the most ancient of Egyptian funerary gods — the jackal-headed guardian of the dead whose dark form has watched over tombs and necropolises since the earliest dynasties.
full_text:
Anubis is the most ancient of Egyptian funerary gods — the jackal-headed guardian of the dead whose dark form has watched over tombs and necropolises since the earliest dynasties. His image appears on the walls of the pyramids, on the coffins of pharaohs, and on the amulets placed over the hearts of the mummified dead. His black skin is not the color of evil but of the fertile Nile soil and of the flesh of the dead, wrapped in linen and preserved for eternity.

According to the Pyramid Texts, Anubis was the son of Osiris and Nephthys. His mother, fearing her husband Set's jealousy, abandoned him at birth. He was found and raised by Isis, his father's devoted wife. This dual parentage gave Anubis a unique role: he belonged to neither the world of the living nor the world of the dead but moved between both.

Anubis presided over the embalming process. It was he who invented mummification, treating the body of his father Osiris with such skill that Osiris became the first mummy and the lord of the underworld. Every Egyptian funeral began with prayers to Anubis, asking him to protect the body and guide the soul through the dangers of the Duat — the dark underworld filled with demons, serpents, and lakes of fire.

But Anubis's most important role came in the Hall of Two Truths. There, before Osiris's throne, the heart of the deceased was placed on a great scale, balanced against the feather of Ma'at. Anubis himself adjusted the counterweight. Thoth, the ibis-headed god of writing, recorded the result. If the heart was lighter than the feather, the soul entered Aaru, the Field of Reeds. But if the heart was heavier, it was devoured by Ammit the Devourer, a creature with the head of a crocodile, the body of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus. That soul ceased to exist, destroyed utterly.

For three thousand years, Anubis watched over the dead of Egypt. He remains the most recognizable figure of Egyptian mythology — the jackal at the gate of eternity, the weigher of hearts, the guardian of the passage every soul must make.
moral: Your deeds are weighed.
source_references: Faulkner, Raymond O. The Ancient Egyptian Book of the Dead. British Museum Publications, 1985; Taylor, John H. Death and the Afterlife in Ancient Egypt. British Museum Press, 2001

title: Arash the Archer
summary: An Iranian hero sacrifices his life force to shoot an arrow that defines the nation's border.
full_text:
In the Basque Country of northern Spain, where the Pyrenees mountains meet the Bay of Biscay and the language spoken is older than any in Europe, there is a being who is both creator and destroyer. Her name is Mari, the Lady of Anboto, and she is the earth goddess of the Basque people.

Mari lives in the caves of the high mountains. She moves from cave to cave, staying in each one for seven years. She rides across the sky on a chariot drawn by four horses, and when she passes, the clouds roil and the winds howl. She is the weather — the storms and the dry spells, the hail and the sunshine.

Mari is a beautiful woman with long golden hair that she combs with a golden comb. But she is also terrifying. When angered, she hurls lightning bolts from the mountains and sends hailstorms to destroy the crops of those who have wronged her. She is the guardian of justice, punishing liars, thieves, and oath-breakers without mercy.

There is a story from the village of Anboto about a farmer who denied shelter to a stranger during a storm. The stranger was Mari in disguise. The next morning, the farmer found his fields covered in hailstones the size of his fist, his cattle dead, his house struck by lightning. He had failed the test of hospitality.

But Mari is also a protector. She rewards those who are kind to strangers, who keep their promises, and who respect the earth. She has a particular fondness for shepherds. A shepherd who leaves a piece of bread and a sip of wine at the mouth of her cave will find his flock healthy and his pastures green.

Mari's husband is Sugaar, a serpent-dragon who lives beneath the earth. They meet on Friday nights to conceive the storms that will sweep across the Basque Country. Their union is the source of both fertility and destruction.

Basque mythology is one of the oldest living mythologies in Europe, predating the arrival of the Indo-European peoples. Mari is not a goddess of a pantheon — she is the goddess, the supreme being of the Basque cosmos. She represents the raw power of nature, the sacredness of the mountains, and the ancient truth that the earth gives and the earth takes away.
moral: True heroism requires sacrifice of oneself for the greater good.

source_references.title: The Shah Nameh
source_references.author: Firdawsi (trans. James Atkinson)
source_references.notes: Arash the Archer's arrow-flight marking Iran's northern border in the Shahnameh
